chart.title: Decadal average: Annual death rate from all natural disasters
chart.subtitle: Death rates are measured as the number of deaths per 100,000 people. Decadal figures are measured as the annual average over the subsequent ten-year period. Disasters include events such as earthquakes, volcanoes, droughts, wildfires, storms, and floods.
chart.note: Based on recorded events; coverage is more limited before the year 2000. Longer historical trends may partly reflect reporting improvements.
chart.citation: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024)

Deaths per 100,000 people - Droughts (de.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from droughts
Deaths per 100,000 people - Droughts (de.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from droughts - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Droughts (de.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

Drought is defined as an extended period of unusually low precipitation that produces a shortage of water for people, animals, and plants. Drought is different from most other hazards in that it develops slowly, sometimes even over the years, and its onset is generally difficult to detect.
Deaths per 100,000 people - Droughts (de.citationShort: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data
Deaths per 100,000 people - Droughts (de.citationLong: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data. “Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from droughts – EM-DAT” [dataset]. EM-DAT, “The International Disasters Database”; Various sources, “Population” [original data].

Deaths per 100,000 people - Earthquakes.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from earthquakes
Deaths per 100,000 people - Earthquakes.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from earthquakes - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Earthquakes.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

An earthquake is defined as a sudden movement of a block of the Earth's crust along a geological fault and associated ground shaking. The data includes the impacts of earthquake events, aftershocks and tsunamis.
Deaths per 100,000 people - Earthquakes.citationShort: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data
Deaths per 100,000 people - Earthquakes.citationLong: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data. “Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from earthquakes – EM-DAT” [dataset]. EM-DAT, “The International Disasters Database”; Various sources, “Population” [original data].

Deaths per 100,000 people - Volcanoes (d.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from volcanoes
Deaths per 100,000 people - Volcanoes (d.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from volcanoes - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Volcanoes (d.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.
Volcanic activity is defined as any type of volcanic event near an opening/vent in the Earth's surface including volcanic eruptions of lava, ash, hot vapor, gas, and pyroclastic material.

Deaths per 100,000 people - Floods (deca.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from floods
Deaths per 100,000 people - Floods (deca.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from floods - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Floods (deca.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

Flood is used as a general term for the overflow of water from a stream channel onto normally dry land in the floodplain (riverine flooding), higher-than-normal levels along the coast (coastal flooding) and in lakes or reservoirs as well as ponding of water at or near the point where the rain fell (flash floods). We also include glacial lake outburst floods in this category.

EM-DAT, “The International Disasters Database”; Various sources, “Population” [original data].

Deaths per 100,000 people - Landslides (.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from landslides
Deaths per 100,000 people - Landslides (.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from landslides - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Landslides (.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

A landslide is the downslope movement of rock, soil, or debris under gravity. This includes both wet mass movements (such as mudflows triggered by heavy rain or snowmelt) and dry mass movements (such as rockfalls).
Deaths per 100,000 people - Landslides (.citationShort: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data

EM-DAT, “The International Disasters Database”; Various sources, “Population” [original data].

Deaths per 100,000 people - Storms (deca.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.
Storms include tornadoes, hailstorms, thunderstorms, sandstorms, blizzards, and extreme wind events.

Deaths per 100,000 people - Wildfires (d.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from wildfires
Deaths per 100,000 people - Wildfires (d.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from wildfires - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Wildfires (d.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

A wildfire is defined as any uncontrolled and non-prescribed combustion or burning of plants in a natural setting such as a forest, grassland, brush land or tundra, which consumes natural fuels and spreads based on environmental conditions (e.g., wind, or topography). Wildfires can be triggered by lightning or human actions.
Deaths per 100,000 people - Wildfires (d.citationShort: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data
Deaths per 100,000 people - Wildfires (d.citationLong: EM-DAT, CRED / UCLouvain (2026); Population based on various sources (2024) – with major processing by Our World in Data. “Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from wildfires – EM-DAT” [dataset]. EM-DAT, “The International Disasters Database”; Various sources, “Population” [original data].

Deaths per 100,000 people - Extreme temp.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from extreme temperatures
Deaths per 100,000 people - Extreme temp.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from extreme temperatures - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - Extreme temp.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

Extreme temperature is used as a general term for temperature variations above (extreme heat) or below (extreme cold) normal conditions. Deaths from extreme temperatures are often indirect, meaning they are not reported or quantified without additional analysis and modelling. Some countries or regions increasingly do this work, but records are very geographically and temporally incomplete. This makes it hard to discern trends over time, or differences between countries.

EM-DAT, “The International Disasters Database”; Various sources, “Population” [original data].

Deaths per 100,000 people - All disaster.titleShort: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from all disasters
Deaths per 100,000 people - All disaster.titleLong: Decadal average: Annual rate of deaths from all disasters - EM-DAT
Deaths per 100,000 people - All disaster.descriptionShort: Death rate per 100,000 people as a result of a natural disaster.
descriptionKey: EM-DAT counts deaths as confirmed deaths, plus missing people.

EM-DAT defines a disaster as a situation or event which overwhelms local capacity, necessitating a request to the national or international level for external assistance; an unforeseen and often sudden event that causes great damage, destruction, and human suffering.

Drought is defined as an extended period of unusually low precipitation that produces a shortage of water for people, animals, and plants. Drought is different from most other hazards in that it develops slowly, sometimes even over the years, and its onset is generally difficult to detect.

An earthquake is defined as a sudden movement of a block of the Earth's crust along a geological fault and associated ground shaking. The data includes the impacts of earthquake events, aftershocks and tsunamis.

Extreme temperature is used as a general term for temperature variations above (extreme heat) or below (extreme cold) normal conditions. Deaths from extreme temperatures are often indirect, meaning they are not reported or quantified without additional analysis and modelling. Some countries or regions increasingly do this work, but records are very geographically and temporally incomplete. This makes it hard to discern trends over time, or differences between countries.
Storms include tornadoes, hailstorms, thunderstorms, sandstorms, blizzards, and extreme wind events.

Flood is used as a general term for the overflow of water from a stream channel onto normally dry land in the floodplain (riverine flooding), higher-than-normal levels along the coast (coastal flooding) and in lakes or reservoirs as well as ponding of water at or near the point where the rain fell (flash floods). We also include glacial lake outburst floods in this category.
Volcanic activity is defined as any type of volcanic event near an opening/vent in the Earth's surface including volcanic eruptions of lava, ash, hot vapor, gas, and pyroclastic material.

A wildfire is defined as any uncontrolled and non-prescribed combustion or burning of plants in a natural setting such as a forest, grassland, brush land or tundra, which consumes natural fuels and spreads based on environmental conditions (e.g., wind, or topography). Wildfires can be triggered by lightning or human actions.

A landslide is the downslope movement of rock, soil, or debris under gravity. This includes both wet mass movements (such as mudflows triggered by heavy rain or snowmelt) and dry mass movements (such as rockfalls).
